tagged_story_for_tts:
Metal’s hidden exhaustion.<[silence:40ms]> Breaks without warning.<[silence:40ms]>

The term material fatigue comes from how metals weaken after repeated stress—like a runner collapsing not from one sprint, but from thousands of steps.<[silence:40ms]>

A paperclip bent back and forth snaps suddenly.<[silence:40ms]> Not because it’s weak, but because each bend leaves microscopic cracks.<[silence:40ms]> These cracks grow silently, like roots splitting rock.<[silence:40ms]> Engineers call this "cyclic loading":<[silence:40ms]> bridges swaying in wind, airplane wings flexing mid-flight, or even a spoon stirring tea too vigorously.<[silence:40ms]> The damage is invisible until failure.<[silence:40ms]>

In 1842, a train axle fractured near Versailles, killing passengers.<[silence:40ms]> Investigators found no flaw in the metal—just years of vibrations.<[silence:40ms]> This led to fatigue testing:<[silence:40ms]> machines that bend, twist, or pound materials millions of times to predict when they’ll fail.<[silence:40ms]> Today, jet turbines endure simulated decades of stress in labs before ever flying.<[silence:40ms]>

Fatigue has no mercy for scale:<[silence:40ms]> a $200 million oil rig collapsed in 1980 when a single weld cracked after 200,000 waves.<[silence:40ms]>

Three related subjects are stress corrosion cracking, fracture mechanics basics, and how bones resist breaking.<[silence:40ms]>
clean_story:
Metal’s hidden exhaustion. Breaks without warning.

The term material fatigue comes from how metals weaken after repeated stress—like a runner collapsing not from one sprint, but from thousands of steps.

A paperclip bent back and forth snaps suddenly. Not because it’s weak, but because each bend leaves microscopic cracks. These cracks grow silently, like roots splitting rock. Engineers call this "cyclic loading": bridges swaying in wind, airplane wings flexing mid-flight, or even a spoon stirring tea too vigorously. The damage is invisible until failure.

In 1842, a train axle fractured near Versailles, killing passengers. Investigators found no flaw in the metal—just years of vibrations. This led to fatigue testing: machines that bend, twist, or pound materials millions of times to predict when they’ll fail. Today, jet turbines endure simulated decades of stress in labs before ever flying.

Fatigue has no mercy for scale: a $200 million oil rig collapsed in 1980 when a single weld cracked after 200,000 waves.

Three related subjects are stress corrosion cracking, fracture mechanics basics, and how bones resist breaking.
